perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that all property, including intellectual property rights, in Confidential Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party shall remain with and be vested in the Disclosing Party or relevant member of the Disclosing Party Group.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that all property, including intellectual property rights, in Confidential Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party shall remain with and be vested in the Disclosing Party or relevant member of the Disclosing Party Group, except for any improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party, which shall be owned by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction. Initially, all intellectual property rights remain with the Disclosing Party. However, the addition states that improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party are owned by the Receiving Party. This contradicts the original statement and creates uncertainty regarding ownership of derivative works.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that all property, including intellectual property rights, in Confidential Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party shall remain with and be vested in the Disclosing Party or relevant member of the Disclosing Party Group, except for any improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party, which shall be owned by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to section 6.1, stating that improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party are owned by the Receiving Party. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text. However, the original text stated that all property, including intellectual property rights, remains with the Disclosing Party. The addition in the Changed Text directly contradicts the original intent and causes confusion. Therefore, the changes to section 6.1 create a direct conflict regarding intellectual property ownership, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time, unless the Disclosing Party provides written notice explicitly terminating these obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states the Receiving Party's obligations continue indefinitely. The added clause allows the Disclosing Party to terminate these obligations with written notice. This creates a contradiction, as the obligations are both perpetual and terminable, leading to uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.3 Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time, unless the Disclosing Party provides written notice explicitly terminating these obligations.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Receiving Party's obligations continue without limit of time. The changed text introduces a condition where the Disclosing Party can terminate these obligations with written notice. This creates a direct contradiction because the obligations are simultaneously defined as perpetual and terminable, leading to uncertainty and inconsistency in enforcement. The contradicted text is the same as the Changed Text, which emphasizes the fact that the introduced change causes a contradiction to the previous understanding.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party and each Receiving Party Authorised Person shall be entitled to disclose Confidential Information to the extent that it is required to do so by applicable law or by order of a court or other public body that has jurisdiction over the Receiving Party or Receiving Party Authorised Person, provided that Clause 4 shall apply to disclosures required under the FOIA or the EIRs.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party and each Receiving Party Authorised Person shall be entitled to disclose Confidential Information to the extent that it is required to do so by applicable law or by order of a court or other public body that has jurisdiction over the Receiving Party or Receiving Party Authorised Person, unless such disclosure would materially harm the Disclosing Party's competitive position; Clause 4 shall apply to disclosures required under the FOIA or the EIRs.
perturbation.explanation: This change inserts a contradiction by stating that disclosure is permitted by law, yet simultaneously restricting it if it harms the Disclosing Party's competitive position. This creates ambiguity about whether legal obligations to disclose outweigh the potential harm to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party and each Receiving Party Authorised Person shall be entitled to disclose Confidential Information to the extent that it is required to do so by applicable law or by order of a court or other public body that has jurisdiction over the Receiving Party or Receiving Party Authorised Person, unless such disclosure would materially harm the Disclosing Party's competitive position; Clause 4 shall apply to disclosures required under the FOIA or the EIRs.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the Receiving Party is entitled to disclose Confidential Information to the extent required by law. The changed text adds a restriction to this entitlement: unless such disclosure would materially harm the Disclosing Party's competitive position. This directly contradicts the original entitlement by imposing a condition that could prevent legally required disclosures, creating a strong in-text contradiction, as the Contradicted text is the same as the Changed text, making the contradiction very clear.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 Any notice to be given under this Agreement (each a “Notice”) shall be given in writing and shall be delivered by hand and shall be deemed to have been duly given at the time of delivery provided that such Notice is sent to the relevant physical address, and expressly marked for the attention of the relevant individual, set out in Clause 7.2.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 Any notice to be given under this Agreement (each a “Notice”) shall be given in writing and shall be delivered by hand or via email to legal@example.com and shall be deemed to have been duly given at the time of delivery provided that such Notice is sent to the relevant physical address, and expressly marked for the attention of the relevant individual, set out in Clause 7.2, or in case of email, upon sending the email.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation adds email as a valid method for delivering notices, which is a contradiction because the phrase after “provided that” limits the delivery exclusively to physical addresses as defined in Clause 7.2, making it uncertain whether the email is valid if addresses aren't found in clause 7.2. This inconsistency leads to potential disputes regarding proper notification.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.1 Any notice to be given under this Agreement (each a “Notice”) shall be given in writing and shall be delivered by hand or via email to legal@example.com and shall be deemed to have been duly given at the time of delivery provided that such Notice is sent to the relevant physical address, and expressly marked for the attention of the relevant individual, set out in Clause 7.2, or in case of email, upon sending the email.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces email as a valid method of notice. The 'provided that' clause, however, restricts valid notice to physical addresses defined in Clause 7.2. The contradiction lies in the fact that the email notice lacks any specification for valid addressing in Clause 7.2, rendering it unclear whether an email notice is valid if physical addresses aren't found in Clause 7.2, creating an inconsistency regarding the proper method of notification. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, implying that the location of the text itself doesn't determine contradiction but rather the change within it.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 Each Party hereby irrevocably submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts in respect of any claim or dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 Each Party hereby irrevocably submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts in respect of any claim or dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, except for disputes relating to intellectual property, which shall be resolved through binding arbitration in New York.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by stating that all disputes fall under the jurisdiction of English courts, but then creates an exception for intellectual property disputes, which must be arbitrated in New York. This conflicting jurisdiction clause creates uncertainty and potential legal challenges regarding the correct venue for dispute resolution.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.2 Each Party hereby irrevocably submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts in respect of any claim or dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, except for disputes relating to intellectual property, which shall be resolved through binding arbitration in New York.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the English courts have exclusive jurisdiction over all disputes. The changed text modifies this by creating an exception for intellectual property disputes, which are to be resolved through binding arbitration in New York. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, and this creates an in-text contradiction because one part of the contract asserts exclusive jurisdiction in one location (English courts) while simultaneously mandating a different dispute resolution mechanism (arbitration in New York) for a specific category of disputes. This inconsistency directly breaks compatibility.
